items.html: Discover the leading cause of mortality and morbidity in SCD
Categorize the different risk factors according to the different stroke subtypes
Identify the different signs and symptoms of stroke
List preventative strategies for strokes in the future

slides.title: According to the Cooperative Study of Sickle Cell Disease, about 11% of children and young adults with Hb SS will develop stroke by 18 years of age and ~25% by 45 years of age

data.html: According to the Cooperative Study of Sickle Cell Disease, about 11% of children and young adults with Hb SS will develop stroke by 18 years of age and ~25% by 45 years of age.

slides.title: What is the stroke risk for children with SCD vs children without SCD?

data.html:
What is the stroke risk for children with SCD vs children without SCD?

A child with SCD has 333 times higher stroke risk!! The risk of stroke in general paediatric population is <1%.

  • About 11% of children and young adults with Hb SS will develop stroke by 18 years of age.
  • Approximately 25% by 45 years of age.

Most common in children 2–5 years of age and adults over 30 years of age. Mostly results from stenosis or occlusion of big vessels in brain. Presentation depends on the location and size of the lesion.

Risk Factors:

  • Transient ischemic attack (TIA)
  • Frequent acute chest syndrome (ACS)
  • ACS within 2 weeks
  • Hypoxia — daytime or nocturnal
  • Presence of high transcranial doppler (TCD) and/or silent cerebral infarct (SCI)

Most common in adults 20–29 years of age. Results from rupture of intracranial aneurysm or collaterals in association of moyamoya syndrome. Present with severe headaches and focal neurologic deficits. Reported mortality 30–50%.

Risk Factors:

  • Older age
  • Low baseline Hb or acute anemia
  • High steady-state WBC
  • Hypertension

Most common neurological entity in patients with SCD and is not age dependent. SCI is defined as abnormal MRI findings of the brain with normal neurological findings, without history of physical findings consistent with overt stroke. ~10–30% of patients will have changes on MRI.

Prevalence of SCI approximately:

  • 27% by 7 years of age
  • 37% by 14 years of age
  • Continues into adult age and is most common in HbSS: 3–38% for Beta Sickle Thalassemia; 5–31% for HbSC

Photo credit: DeBaun, M et al., 2012
Recognizing the signs and symptoms of stroke is essential for prompt intervention. The images below illustrate key warning signs to watch for in patients with SCD.

items.html: Affects major arteries of brain
Endothelial Activation → endothelial damage and hyperplasia → Stenosis
Repeated sickling → intraluminal thrombosis → Occlusion
Thinning of blood vessels may lead to aneurysm – present in approximately 10% of patients with SCD

data.html: Thinning of blood vessels may lead to aneurysm – present in around 10% of patients with SCD. Thinning of blood vessels may lead to aneurysm – present in around 10% of patients with SCD.

Risk Factors Specific to SCD for Stroke

Hb SC patients are at lower risk of stroke compared to Hb SS patients. Lifetime risk of stroke is >3%, but the risk is still 50–100 times higher than healthy Black children. Brazilian data suggest baseline lower TAMMV velocities for HbSC patients.

Risk for stroke in other double heterozygous genotypes (HbSD, HB SE, Hb beta thal, etc.) depends on patient's:

  • Baseline hemoglobin
  • Evidence of hemolysis
  • Severity of SCD

Preventative Maintenance

  • Annual TCD starts from 2 years of age through 16 years of age.
  • For patients with borderline TAMMV velocity – repeat in 3–6 months.
  • For patients with high TAMMV velocity – do MRI along with MRA and MRV.
  • No blood transfusion in 3 months prior to TCD Testing.
  • Patient preferably in their baseline health status.

What is the significance of abnormal TAMMV (≥200 cm/s) in TCD Ultrasonography?

It is strongly associated with a higher risk of stroke.

Explanation: Abnormal TAMMV (≥200 cm/s) in TCD Ultrasonography is strongly associated with a higher risk of stroke.

What is the recommended frequency for annual TCD screening in stroke risk screening?

Annually.

Explanation: Annual TCD screening is recommended as part of stroke risk screening.

items.html: The highest risk for stroke is during the first decade, esp. during 2–5 years of age.
Risk factors for stroke in SCD include: Hemoglobin level, amount of Hb S, CNS vasculopathy, increased oxygen demand, prior cerebral infarct
Transcranial doppler is a non-invasive and rapid test that is used for screening SCD patients at high risk for stroke.
